message:
fix(tantivy): unify frankensqlite-unsupported errors via shared helper citing cargo feature flag

ft-lzbkn: tantivy_ingest.rs:819 and tantivy_reindex.rs:52 both returned
IndexerError::Config with nearly-but-not-identical strings when the
frankensqlite event source was selected. Applies the fe0e2ca3 pattern
to the downstream stubs: a single `frankensqlite_unsupported(context)`
helper returns a consistent, discoverable message that names the
`frankensqlite-recorder` cargo feature so operators know how to enable
support when it lands.

Also closes ft-lzbkn.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-eze48","title":"[fuzz] add FtviIndex::from_bytes harness for vector index loader","description":"The FTVI vector-index binary loader is public and currently unfuzzed.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/mod.rs:73` publicly re-exports `FtviIndex`.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:218-267` parses attacker-controlled bytes in `FtviIndex::from_bytes`, including magic/version checks, dimension/count headers, and per-record half-float decoding loops.
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no target for `FtviIndex::from_bytes`.

Why it matters:
- This is a binary format loader over persisted search state, so malformed bytes can hit allocation pressure, unexpected EOF handling, half-float edge cases, and count/dimension loops.
- The surface is already public, synchronous, and easy to fuzz without extra seams.

Fix:
- Add a fuzz target that exercises both arbitrary raw bytes and structured valid FTVI buffers via `write_ftvi_vec` round-trips, asserting parse no-panics and round-trip sanity where applicable.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","estimated_minutes":30,"created_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331896Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.556262Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.555662Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 6567397b: add FtviIndex::from_bytes fuzz harness","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","review"]}
-{"id":"ft-f11v6","title":"[MEDIUM] no semantic_schema_version marker; vector store not invalidated on storage schema migration","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:28:11.955675Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T19:22:24.790355Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review","search","storage"],"comments":[{"id":3639,"issue_id":"ft-f11v6","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Gap

`ft_meta` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs, wa_meta→ft_meta rename
at line 4714) tracks the main storage schema version via `user_version`
and `record_migration`. The vector store in
`crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/chunk_vector_store.rs` tracks its own
`lexical_schema_version` per generation (line 57) but nothing ties the
two together.

There is NO `semantic_schema_version` / `vector_store_version` row in
ft_meta. If storage.rs migrations change the semantic contract
(for example: change how segment ordinals are assigned, change the
content_hash function, bump the chunk policy implicitly), the vector
store silently retains the old embeddings indexed under the old contract
and continues serving them from `semantic_search`.

Today's scenario is benign because migrations v18/v19/v21 don't touch
the semantic chunk surface. But the guardrail is missing.

# Why this matters

When storage schema bumps, the semantic store should either:
(a) be invalidated (DELETE all rows, mark generation Failed, force reindex),
(b) be version-gated (writes after bump must use the new contract, reads
    from old rows are filtered), or
(c) explicitly documented as schema-independent with a test that freezes
    the coupling.

None of (a)/(b)/(c) is present.

# Shape of fix

Smallest defensive move (≤30 LOC, safe to land):

* Add `semantic_schema_version INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1` column to
  `semantic_generations` via a new migration.
* On orchestrator startup, if a generation's stored version < current
  const `SEMANTIC_SCHEMA_VERSION`, mark it inactive or Failed with a
  reason string and force a rebuild.

Larger defensive move: add a `semantic_schema_version` row to `ft_meta`
and enforce match at generation register time.

# Out of scope

Actually migrating the contract. This bead is the version-marker only,
so future contract changes HAVE a graceful invalidation path.","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:28:28Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-icreu","title":"[MEDIUM] ActivityProfile::new allows NaN alpha/default_activity past clamp; poisons all hourly_ewma slots","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/survival.rs:437-438` — `ActivityProfile::new`.

ft-26k3h just hardened `update_hour` against NaN input. The construction-time path is the **complement**: `alpha.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` and `default_activity.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` both still let NaN through because `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN.

Two independent failure modes:

1. **NaN `default_activity`**: all 24 `hourly_ewma` slots are initialised to NaN. `predict_hour` returns NaN for every slot until the first clean `update_hour` overwrites the bucket via the `sample_count == 0` branch. Until then, every hazard/survival score computed from `predict` silently produces NaN (matches the ft-26k3h failure shape but from a different path).

2. **NaN `alpha`**: `self.alpha = NaN`. `update_hour` at line 460 computes `alpha.mul_add(sample, (1.0 - alpha) * prev) = NaN` on every non-first sample. `hourly_ewma[idx]` stays NaN from the second sample onward. ft-26k3h's entry guard doesn't catch this because the caller passes a clean `normalized_activity` — the NaN is stored in `self.alpha`, not in the input.

Both are the same `f64::clamp(NaN)` gotcha shipped across ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5 / ft-x50ce / ft-26k3h. This closes the pair's construction-time counterpart.

## Fix

Snap NaN to sensible defaults at the boundary:
- `alpha` → 0.1 (a conservative EWMA coefficient; config-adjacent code uses 0.3 as the common default but 0.1 is safe for an unspecified sentinel).
- `default_activity` → 0.5 (neutral midpoint — if operator didn't supply a meaningful default, 0.5 is the least-surprising fallback).

Both `.is_nan()` checks happen **before** the clamp so the clamp still trims out-of-range finite inputs normally.

## Tests

* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_alpha` — alpha=NaN → stored α=0.1, EWMA updates stay finite.
* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_default_activity` — default=NaN → all hourly_ewma slots = 0.5, predict_hour returns 0.5.

## Scope

Single file (`survival.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:55.543754Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995504Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ih4tm","title":"[MEDIUM] defer_fts_triggers=false is not reversible: triggers stay dropped after enabling once (ft-wk5fo follow-up)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`.

d74401c6 landed `StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers` (ft-wk5fo). The intent is a reversible opt-in: flip to `true` to trade FTS freshness for write-path latency, flip back to `false` to restore synchronous indexing. The inline comment at line 6006 says as much:

> The triggers can be re-created by re-opening with `defer_fts_triggers: false`

That claim is wrong. Concrete interleaving:

1. Operator opens DB with `defer_fts_triggers: true` on an up-to-date schema (`user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION`).
2. `initialize_schema` returns early at line 4315 (no SQL executed, up-to-date path).
3. The explicit `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS output_segments_ai/ad/au` at line 6010 runs — triggers gone.
4. Operator flips config to `defer_fts_triggers: false` and restarts.
5. `initialize_schema` again short-circuits at line 4315.
6. Line 6010 is skipped (flag is false). **No `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS` runs.**
7. Database silently stays in deferred mode; the operator's `false` setting is a no-op.

The inline comment at line 6007 hints the author knew: 'this commit ships the opt-in direction only; reverting would require re-running SCHEMA_SQL or the operator dropping the DB.' That matches reality but contradicts the line-6006 'can be re-created by re-opening' claim — and leaves the config behaving non-intuitively for operators.

## Severity rationale

- Not a crash or data loss.
- Silent wrong answer: operator flips back to `false`, expects synchronous FTS, gets deferred.
- One-way-door config is a known anti-pattern. Easy fix.

## Fix sketch

Make the open path truly bidirectional. On every open, after `initialize_schema` returns:

* If `defer_fts_triggers == true`: `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS ...` (idempotent).
* If `defer_fts_triggers == false`: `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS ...` using the same SQL the SCHEMA_SQL block defines.

Factor the trigger SQL into one private const referenced by both branches so SCHEMA_SQL and the re-create branch cannot drift.

## Tests

* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_roundtrip — open with true, verify triggers dropped, close, reopen with false, verify triggers re-created.
* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_idempotent_reopen — open with same flag twice back-to-back, no spurious SQL errors, trigger state stable.

## Scope

Single file (`storage.rs`). No API break (`defer_fts_triggers` already public, default `false`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:12.433144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095316Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095017Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ismux","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything. This also makes the existing close reason on `ft-y9lnb.3` overstated relative to the current code.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:05:13.673841Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:13:49.575203Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:13:49.574685Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-4r2ib. crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs is already dirty in this checkout with the exact handle_detection/WorkflowStartResult fix and regression coverage, so I did not collide with pane-4-owned WIP.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-lokam","title":"[LOW] diag bundle environment.json leaks absolute workspace cwd; tracing fields leak operator paths","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #6 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Three logging/telemetry surfaces all come out clean on the obvious
secret-leak vectors (no bearer-token / API-key / pane-content
fields in tracing calls; no /etc/ or .ssh/ reads in diag
collection). One defense-in-depth gap remains: path privacy.

## Findings

### F1 [LOW] diag bundle environment.json includes absolute cwd
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/diagnostic.rs:87-89
  ```rust
  cwd: std::env::current_dir()
      .ok()
      .map(|p| p.display().to_string()),
  ```
- Diag bundles (`ft diag bundle`) are often attached to bug reports
  and shared outside the host. An absolute cwd reveals:
  - the user's home path segment (`/Users/<username>/...` or
    `/home/<username>/...`),
  - the project path (`/Users/alice/projects/secret-startup-pitch-deck/`),
  - on some setups, mount points that hint at filesystem layout.
- Severity: LOW. Users implicitly share the workspace when
  debugging, but the current behavior removes the choice.

### F2 [LOW] tracing fields log operator-private paths
- Multiple `tracing::[info|warn|debug]!(..., path = %X.display(), ...)`
  call sites scatter socket paths, DB paths, and workspace paths
  through structured logs. Representative hits (non-exhaustive):
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs:258, 302, 331,
      362, 401 — unix socket paths.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:1118 —
      `info_span!("telemetry_store_open", path = %db_path.display())`.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs:1375, 2658, 2814 —
      socket paths on accept failures.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/build_coord.rs:265 —
      `meta_path = %self.meta_path.display()`.
- Severity: LOW. These logs land in the user's local log file,
  not network-visible by default, but they do end up in diag
  bundles when `collect_recent_log_lines` is called.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- Bearer tokens in structured logs: grep across
  `tracing::{info,debug,warn,error}!` call sites for
  `token|bearer|secret|password|api_key` returned zero production
  hits. Clean.
- Pane content in tracing fields: no production code logs
  `segment.content`, `delta_text`, or `pane_text`. Only test
  `assert_eq!(segment.content, ...)` references found. Clean.
- Diag bundle reading privileged files: no `/etc/`, `.ssh/`,
  `.aws/`, `.gnupg/` reads in `diagnostic.rs`. The bundle only
  reads the ft SQLite DB (already redacted via `redact_events`)
  and writes to the workspace-local `diag_dir`. Clean.
- Environment variables in diag bundle: `gather_environment`
  captures only OS / arch / rust_version / wa_version / cwd —
  no `std::env::vars()` dump. Clean.
- Summarize_config: the docstring already calls out "no paths or
  secrets" and the implementation confirms — only numeric/boolean
  config values are copied over. Clean.

## Fix direction
- F1: replace absolute cwd with just the basename, or gate
  inclusion behind `DiagnosticOptions.include_cwd: bool` default
  `false`. Concretely at diagnostic.rs:87:
  ```rust
  cwd: if opts.include_cwd {
      std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|p| p.display().to_string())
  } else {
      std::env::current_dir()
          .ok()
          .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
  },
  ```
- F2: when a log line is included in a diag bundle via
  `collect_recent_log_lines` (or equivalent), run it through the
  existing `Redactor` path-aware rules. Non-bundle in-process logs
  stay as-is since they aid operator debugging on the local box.

## Severity rationale
- LOW (P2): neither finding reveals a secret, and both sit behind
  an operator action (`ft diag bundle` + sharing it). The fix
  shrinks the unintentional-share footprint but doesn't close a
  live exploit.

## Related
- ft-ybtyg (598a7c13) — snapshot/checkpoint hashing honesty.
- ft-2guux (doc reality-check) — complementary; this bead is the
  diagnostics-exfil counterpart.

 {"id":"ft-ltpog","title":"native_events: decode errors logged at debug, invisible in production","description":"# [MEDIUM] native_events: malformed wire events logged at debug

**Subsystem:** native_events / protocol_recovery
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs (`handle_connection_with_cx`)

## The hole

When wire-event decoding fails (invalid JSON, bad base64, unknown event
type), the handler emitted:

\`\`\`rust
Err(err) => {
    debug!(error = %err, "failed to decode native event (cx path)");
}
\`\`\`

A malformed wire event is a protocol anomaly — version skew between
frankenterm and the mux-server, on-wire corruption, or a hostile peer
writing into the native-events socket. Operators routinely filter
`debug!` in production, so the signal was effectively lost.

Contrast with the `MAX_EVENT_LINE_BYTES` path a few lines above, which
already emits at `warn!`. Same class of anomaly, inconsistent level.

## Fix

Promoted the decode-error log to `warn!`. The `warn!` import was already
present — no dependency change.

Future work (separate bead): attach a telemetry counter for
decode-failure rate so it can drive a SLI rather than relying solely on
log sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:39.950825Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568904Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568595Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["native-events","observability"]}

 {"id":"ft-m9gob","title":"robot_envelope::canonicalize_json panics on huge non-integer f64 via (float * 10000) overflow to infinity","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/robot_envelope.rs:48-57`:

```rust
Value::Number(number) => {
    if let Some(float) = number.as_f64() {
        if float.fract() != 0.0 {
            let rounded = (float * 10_000.0).round() / 10_000.0;
            *value = Value::Number(
                serde_json::Number::from_f64(rounded).expect("rounded float"),
            );
        }
    }
}
```

For any non-integer f64 where `|float|` is above roughly `1.8e304`, the multiplication `float * 10_000.0` overflows to `f64::INFINITY`. `Infinity.round() / 10_000.0` remains Infinity. `serde_json::Number::from_f64(Infinity)` returns `None` (JSON disallows non-finite numbers). The unconditional `.expect("rounded float")` then PANICS the caller.

## Concrete trigger

A robot envelope payload that deserializes a number like `1e305` (valid JSON, fits in f64, finite) — e.g., a bridge that surfaces a cosmic-scale metric, a misconfigured tuning value, or an adversarial payload — passes through `canonicalize_json` on its way to the golden-file path. `1e305 * 10_000 = 1e309` → INFINITY → panic.

The bug is reachable:
1. From trusted internal bridges that happen to emit large-but-finite f64 values.
2. From any test-fixture or dev tool that round-trips JSON through canonicalize_json without sanitizing numeric ranges.

## Fix

Two options, both safe:
1. Short-circuit: `if !float.is_finite() { return; }` at the top of the Number arm. The caller's original (finite but huge) value is preserved — no canonicalization but no panic.
2. Fallback: replace `.expect` with a match that falls back to the original number on None. Same observable behavior, defense-in-depth against any future non-finite source.

Combine both: skip canonicalization when the ORIGINAL float is non-finite OR the ROUNDED product is non-finite. Otherwise pass through unchanged (so the Value keeps the caller's original number, just un-rounded).

## Tests

- `canonicalize_json_preserves_huge_finite_float_without_panicking`: wrap a Value with a 1e305 float under a generic payload, call canonicalize_json, assert it returns without panic.
- `canonicalize_json_preserves_edge_floats`: f64::MAX as non-integer, a denormal value, verify no panic and the original finite value is preserved when the rounded value would overflow.

## Severity

MEDIUM — not a crash on the hot path, but canonicalize_json runs on every robot envelope through a golden-file comparator and every contract test. A single payload with a big f64 takes down the subprocess + anyone running the golden tests. Fix is 3 lines.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:51:09.506491Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:15.285120Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:15.284826Z","close_reason":"Landed in f93753e3 (fix(robot_envelope): guard canonicalize_json against huge-float rounding overflow)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"ft-nyvyl","title":"[HIGH] mux TLS listener accept() has no handshake timeout → slow-loris wedges per-domain accept loop","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:39:36.728112Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T19:22:26.845906Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review","runtime","security"],"comments":[{"id":3640,"issue_id":"ft-nyvyl","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Gap

crates/frankenterm-mux-server/src/ossl.rs:80-109:

    match stream {
        Ok(stream) => {
            stream.set_nodelay(true).ok();
            let acceptor = self.acceptor.clone();
            let dispatch_config = self.dispatch_config;
            match acceptor.accept(stream) {
                Ok(stream) => { ... spawn async dispatch ... }
                Err(e) => log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e),
            }
        }
        ...
    }

Two problems compound:

1. `stream` is a `std::net::TcpStream` and nothing calls
   `set_read_timeout()` / `set_write_timeout()` on it before
   `acceptor.accept(stream)`. The OpenSSL handshake reads/writes on the
   raw blocking socket.
2. `acceptor.accept(stream)` is executed SYNCHRONOUSLY on the accept
   thread, not the async runtime. A peer that opens a TCP socket and
   refuses to advance the TLS handshake (or drip-feeds it a byte at a
   time) parks this thread indefinitely.

Because the accept loop iterates after accept() returns, a single slow /
malicious peer halts every subsequent legitimate connection for this
TLS domain.

# Comparison with client side

frankenterm/client/src/client.rs:1266-1267 does call `set_read_timeout`
and `set_write_timeout` on the client-side TCP stream before initiating
the handshake — so the operator-side connection is protected, but the
server-side isn't.

# Why this matters

This is a zero-credential DoS against the mux-server TLS domain. Any
peer that can reach the TLS port (even with an invalid cert — note
that peer-cert verification only runs AFTER accept() at line 88) can
wedge the listener.

The `set_read_timeout` landing was partial: ft-kovt3 wired it for the
client; this bead extends it to the mux-server accept path, which is
the remaining half.

# Shape of fix (≤30 LOC)

Before `acceptor.accept(stream)` in ossl.rs, set a reasonable handshake
deadline on the raw TCP stream, then clear it once the handshake
completes and before wrapping in AsyncSslStream:

    stream.set_read_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    stream.set_write_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    let stream = match acceptor.accept(stream) {
        Ok(s) => s,
        Err(e) => { log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e); continue; }
    };
    stream.get_ref().set_read_timeout(None).ok();
    stream.get_ref().set_write_timeout(None).ok();

A constant like `const HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT: Duration = Duration::from_secs(10);`
is conservative and aligned with OpenSSL session ticket recommendations.

The `continue` (rather than `break`) on accept failure is also worth a
second look while the file is open — the current `break` at line 90 for
verify_peer_cert errors exits the whole accept loop, which is a
separate DoS lever.

# Out of scope

The select-arm-drop / registration concern raised in the audit is
working as designed (the stream outlives the dropped future; the
IoRegistration lives on the stream, not on the dropped future). No bead.

The capture→relay→persistence shutdown ordering in runtime.rs is also
correct: capture.await completes before relay.await, and relay closes
the ring BEFORE persistence.await is entered (runtime.rs:4124-4141).
No bead.

+{"id":"ft-nyvyl","title":"[HIGH] mux TLS listener accept() has no handshake timeout → slow-loris wedges per-domain accept loop","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:39:36.728112Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T19:25:59.293234Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T19:25:59.292972Z","close_reason":"Shipped in ed413bd2: bound mux TLS handshakes to 5s","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review","runtime","security"],"comments":[{"id":3640,"issue_id":"ft-nyvyl","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Gap

crates/frankenterm-mux-server/src/ossl.rs:80-109:

    match stream {
        Ok(stream) => {
            stream.set_nodelay(true).ok();
            let acceptor = self.acceptor.clone();
            let dispatch_config = self.dispatch_config;
            match acceptor.accept(stream) {
                Ok(stream) => { ... spawn async dispatch ... }
                Err(e) => log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e),
            }
        }
        ...
    }

Two problems compound:

1. `stream` is a `std::net::TcpStream` and nothing calls
   `set_read_timeout()` / `set_write_timeout()` on it before
   `acceptor.accept(stream)`. The OpenSSL handshake reads/writes on the
   raw blocking socket.
2. `acceptor.accept(stream)` is executed SYNCHRONOUSLY on the accept
   thread, not the async runtime. A peer that opens a TCP socket and
   refuses to advance the TLS handshake (or drip-feeds it a byte at a
   time) parks this thread indefinitely.

Because the accept loop iterates after accept() returns, a single slow /
malicious peer halts every subsequent legitimate connection for this
TLS domain.

# Comparison with client side

frankenterm/client/src/client.rs:1266-1267 does call `set_read_timeout`
and `set_write_timeout` on the client-side TCP stream before initiating
the handshake — so the operator-side connection is protected, but the
server-side isn't.

# Why this matters

This is a zero-credential DoS against the mux-server TLS domain. Any
peer that can reach the TLS port (even with an invalid cert — note
that peer-cert verification only runs AFTER accept() at line 88) can
wedge the listener.

The `set_read_timeout` landing was partial: ft-kovt3 wired it for the
client; this bead extends it to the mux-server accept path, which is
the remaining half.

# Shape of fix (≤30 LOC)

Before `acceptor.accept(stream)` in ossl.rs, set a reasonable handshake
deadline on the raw TCP stream, then clear it once the handshake
completes and before wrapping in AsyncSslStream:

    stream.set_read_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    stream.set_write_timeout(Some(HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT))?;
    let stream = match acceptor.accept(stream) {
        Ok(s) => s,
        Err(e) => { log::error!("failed TlsAcceptor: {}", e); continue; }
    };
    stream.get_ref().set_read_timeout(None).ok();
    stream.get_ref().set_write_timeout(None).ok();

A constant like `const HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT: Duration = Duration::from_secs(10);`
is conservative and aligned with OpenSSL session ticket recommendations.

The `continue` (rather than `break`) on accept failure is also worth a
second look while the file is open — the current `break` at line 90 for
verify_peer_cert errors exits the whole accept loop, which is a
separate DoS lever.

# Out of scope

The select-arm-drop / registration concern raised in the audit is
working as designed (the stream outlives the dropped future; the
IoRegistration lives on the stream, not on the dropped future). No bead.

The capture→relay→persistence shutdown ordering in runtime.rs is also
correct: capture.await completes before relay.await, and relay closes
the ring BEFORE persistence.await is entered (runtime.rs:4124-4141).
No bead.

The prune_segments_sync / append_segment_sync race is bounded by
SQLite's single-writer rule on the DB file; the fts_pane_progress
rewind runs inside the same implicit transaction as the DELETE
(storage.rs:14157-14196). No bead.

The ft-nrbrp Cancelled-collapse pattern is already handled in the
relay loop via explicit RecvEvent::{Item, Closed, Cancelled} match
(runtime.rs:2986-3010). No follow-up bead.","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:40:04Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-o657v","title":"conformance_mcp_manifest: pin tool input_schema additionalProperties:false invariant","description":"# Gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` rejects unknown top-level fields (via `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`), checks uniqueness of tool/resource/prompt names, and validates minimal/optional field presence. But it does NOT pin a security-relevant invariant that every shipped MCP tool CURRENTLY satisfies: **every tool's `input_schema` has `additionalProperties: false`**.

Verified by:

    $ rg -c 'additionalProperties' crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs
    29
    $ rg -c 'input_schema:' crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs
    29

All 29 input_schemas currently set it. Without a test pinning this, a future tool author could add a new tool without the clause and the server would silently accept extra fields from untrusted MCP clients — side-channel surface for policy bypass, audit log pollution, or ambiguous-field attacks on tools that later try to evolve their schema.

# Fix

Add `every_tool_input_schema_denies_additional_properties` in conformance_mcp_manifest.rs that iterates `server.tools()` from `build_server_with_db(&Config::default(), Some(...))` and asserts each `tool.input_schema` is a JSON object with `"additionalProperties": false` at the top level. If a tool's schema is NOT type=object (e.g. `{}`, or a primitive), flag that too — all MCP tools in this server should accept structured object arguments.

# Non-fix

Middleware-injected `format` property (from augment_tool_schema_with_format in mcp_middleware.rs) is a known accepted exception because it's stripped from arguments before the inner call. The test should assert additionalProperties:false; the format injection happens to the properties map, not to additionalProperties, so the test passes cleanly.

# Severity

MEDIUM — no live defect; every current tool sets the bit. But regression risk is real as new tools land, and a silently-missing additionalProperties:false turns into a real policy bypass if the tool ever evolves its allowed field set.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:03:57.448008Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:52.683351Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:52.683105Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of f73e139a (absorbed commit). every_tool_input_schema_denies_additional_properties iterates server.tools() from build_server_with_db + asserts each input_schema is type=object with additionalProperties literal false — pins the 29/29 invariant so future tools without the clause break CI instead of silently widening the accepted field set.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-yj375","title":"[security][P1] wa.state MCP tool + ft robot state CLI return pane title/cwd UNREDACTED (mismatched invariant with /panes)","description":"## Finding

Audit under ft-h8da2 (docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md) confirms: wa.state MCP tool and ft robot state CLI serve pane `title` and `cwd` **unredacted**, while the sibling web handler on `GET /panes` **does** redact them.

### Evidence

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:817-834 — McpPaneState::from_pane_info:

      title: info.title.clone(),
      cwd: info.cwd.clone(),

- crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:5501-5518 — PaneState::from_pane_info (CLI): same pattern, same unredacted clone.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/handlers.rs:64-70 — PaneView::from_record (web): **CORRECT** reference implementation:

      title: r.title.map(|t| redactor.redact(&t)),
      cwd: r.cwd.map(|c| redactor.redact(&c)),

### Threat

Pane titles are set by whatever process the pane hosts. Agents that bake credentials into the title (e.g. `codex --api-key sk-ant-...` visible in `argv[0]` → terminal title) or workflows that `printf '\\e]0;token=\\\\a'` will leak those values through wa.state and ft robot state.

A peer MCP agent that calls wa.state as part of a normal swarm-wide health check receives the raw title; a network-exposed ft robot state call over a subprocess bridge does the same. Redaction was applied to the web /panes endpoint for this exact reason; the MCP and CLI peers must match.

### Fix

Two equivalent options:

Option A (one-line per call-site, web-matching): in the wa.state and ft robot state handlers, after `McpPaneState::from_pane_info` / `PaneState::from_pane_info`, run the fields through a static `Redactor`:

    let redactor = Redactor::new();
    for s in &mut states {
        if let Some(t) = s.title.as_mut() { *t = redactor.redact(t); }
        if let Some(c) = s.cwd.as_mut()   { *c = redactor.redact(c); }
    }

Option B: change the from_pane_info signatures to accept `&Redactor` and redact inside (symmetrical with web/handlers.rs:64-70).

Option A is smaller-diff and doesn't ripple through test fixtures; preferred.

### Acceptance Criteria

- wa.state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- ft robot state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- Regression test: seed a pane with title containing `sk-ant-api03-XXXX`, invoke both surfaces, assert the raw token does not appear in the response JSON.
- docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md wa.state + ft robot state rows flip from `✗ LEAK` to `✓`.

### Why P1

Real leak on the default configuration — no operator opt-in required. Blast radius is narrow (titles/cwds, not pane scrollback) but automatic (fleet-wide state poll). Fix is mechanical.

Surfaced by RusticMeadow while landing ft-h8da2's audit matrix, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:28:41.743939Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:40:33.038204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:40:33.037749Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 4d26269f: wa.state and ft robot state redact title/cwd before serialization","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","cli","mcp","redaction","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-yjt9e","title":"policy::RateLimiter::pane_counts leaks entries forever — no eviction on pane destroy or GC path","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2076` — `RateLimiter.pane_counts: HashMap<(u64, ActionKind), Vec<Instant>>`. Every unique `(pane_id, action)` pair `check()` sees creates an entry. Nothing removes them.

A grep confirms it:

    $ rg 'pane_counts\.(remove|clear|retain)|fn gc|fn cleanup|fn prune_panes' crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs
    (no matches)

## Failure shape

On a long-running ft watcher with high pane churn (the 200+-pane fleet the project is designed for), every closed pane leaves a stale `(pane_id, action)` entry in `pane_counts` — one per ActionKind the pane ever touched. Over weeks those entries accumulate:

- Each entry holds a `Vec<Instant>` of up to `limit_per_pane` entries (default 60, configurable up to u32::MAX).
- After the sliding window expires, `prune_old` empties the Vec on the next `check()` — but only if check() is called with that (pane_id, action) again, which never happens for a destroyed pane.
- The entry itself is never removed, so the HashMap grows monotonically.

For 10k destroyed panes × 5 action kinds × 16B-per-Instant × 60-entries-peak = ~48 MB of leaked state. Not a crash, but a silent drift that scales with fleet uptime.

Global side (`global_counts: HashMap<ActionKind, Vec<Instant>>`) is BOUNDED by the finite ActionKind enum — not affected.

# Fix

Add two method shapes to `impl RateLimiter`:

1. `remove_pane(&mut self, pane_id: u64)` — iterate pane_counts, retain only keys whose first element ≠ pane_id. Called from the runtime's PaneDestroyed handler (same handler that already calls `remove_runtime_pane_state_for_pane` in runtime.rs:3574).

2. `gc(&mut self)` (or `gc_at(&mut self, now: Instant)` for testability) — iterate pane_counts, for each entry call prune_old with now-window as cutoff, drop the whole entry if the resulting Vec is empty. Safe opportunistic cleanup that catches panes closed without an explicit PaneDestroyed signal (crashes, abrupt detaches).

Also add operational telemetry: `pane_entries_evicted: u64` counter so operators can see the leak being managed.

# Tests

Inline `#[cfg(test)]` in policy.rs:

- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane`
- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_leaves_other_panes_intact`
- `rate_limiter_gc_drops_expired_empty_entries`
- `rate_limiter_gc_preserves_still-active_entries`

# Severity

MEDIUM — leak is slow, bounded per pane, doesn't panic. But it's cumulative and silent. Fleet operators would only see the symptom as gradual RSS creep over uptime-weeks, attributed to anything-but-rate-limiter. Shipping the eviction path closes the hole before someone has to bisect it from production.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.703056Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.104139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.103827Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of 27ef45e1 (absorbed commit includes ft-yjt9e methods: RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count + 4 regression tests: remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane, remove_pane_leaves_global_counts_intact, gc_drops_expired_empty_entries, gc_preserves_still_active_entries). Lib compiles clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-yskcu","title":"continuous_backpressure EwmaSmoother + sigmoid NaN poison cascade","description":"## Defect

Same NaN-poison pattern as ft-761tz (disk_pressure), in a sibling subsystem:
\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\`.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`ContinuousBackpressure::update(capture_ratio, write_ratio)\` where
   one ratio is \`NaN\`. Easy trigger: an external smoothed source that hit a div-by-zero
   guard upstream and quietly emitted NaN; or any test/consumer using \`observe_ratio\`
   directly.
2. Line 225-226: \`capture_ratio.clamp(0.0, 1.0)\` returns NaN — \`f64::clamp\` propagates
   NaN by design.
3. Line 228: \`self.capture_smoother.update(NaN)\`. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105)
   has **no NaN guard** — \`alpha.mul_add(NaN, ...)\` → NaN → \`self.value = NaN\`
   permanently. Every subsequent \`update()\` call preserves the NaN.
4. Line 231: \`smoothed_capture.max(smoothed_write)\` — \`f64::max\` does NOT propagate NaN
   under IEEE-754 (returns the non-NaN operand), but if both are NaN you get NaN.
5. Line 232-236: \`severity(NaN, _, _)\` → calls local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) which does
   \`x.clamp(-500.0, 500.0)\` — same NaN-propagation footgun. Returns NaN.
6. Line 232: \`self.current_severity = NaN\` permanently.
7. \`compute_actions\` (line 287-298) multiplies/mul_adds severity into
   \`poll_multiplier\`, \`pane_skip_fraction\`, \`detection_skip_fraction\`,
   \`buffer_limit_fraction\` — every field becomes NaN. ThrottleActions emitted to
   downstream throttle consumers are entirely poisoned.
8. \`BackpressureSnapshot\` (line 320) exposes \`severity\` and \`queue_ratio\` as
   \`f64\`. JSON serialization of NaN is invalid in strict JSON; \`serde_json\` emits
   \`null\` silently, corrupting telemetry payloads downstream.
9. Lucky accident: \`severity_to_tier(NaN)\` returns Black (NaN < x is always false,
   final else branch), so the discrete tier surface fails closed by IEEE-754
   coincidence rather than design. The continuous severity surface fails OPEN.

## Severity rationale

- Same class of silent wrong answer as ft-761tz.
- Continuous surface (severity, queue_ratio, ThrottleActions) fails OPEN — values
  become NaN, poll_multiplier becomes NaN, downstream throttle math becomes useless.
- Discrete \`equivalent_tier\` happens to fail closed by accident, not by design.
- Telemetry consumers (snapshot serialization) silently lose data.

## Fix sketch

1. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105) — if \`raw.is_nan()\`, return \`self.value\`
   without state change. Same defense as disk_pressure \`EwmaEstimator::update\`.
2. Local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) — short-circuit \`is_nan()\` to \`0.5\` like the
   backpressure_severity sibling does.
3. \`ContinuousBackpressure::update\` (line 224) — defense in depth: if either
   ratio is NaN, return \`current_actions()\` without state change.
4. Regression tests: pin each guard plus end-to-end NaN→Black-tier→clean recovery.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. No changes to \`backpressure.rs\` or \`backpressure_severity.rs\`
